findings.description:
Unconditional zero return silences division-by-zero for non-zero numerators: When denominator is zero and numerator is non-zero, the function returns 0.0 instead of the IEEE 754 result (Inf or -Inf). For numerator 0.0 / 0.0, it returns 0.0 instead of NaN. Both behaviors replace a well-defined floating-point error signal with an arbitrary sentinel value that downstream code may misinterpret.
Evidence: Diff line: `if denominator == 0.0 { return 0.0; }` — the guard does not distinguish numerator cases.

Exact zero check does not protect against underflow or near-zero overflow: The comparison `denominator == 0.0` catches exact zero (and -0.0) but will not catch extremely small non-zero values such as f64::MIN_POSITIVE, which can still produce Inf when used as a divisor with sufficiently large numerators.
Evidence: Diff line: `if denominator == 0.0` — equality on f64 misses values that are epsilon-close to zero.
